business_info: Within internal analytics reports, the term "Premier League" is sometimes used loosely to refer to any top-division competition in a given country, especially when normalizing club performance across nations. However, in regulatory or contract data, "Premier League" strictly means the English Premier League (league_name = 'England Premier League').

Historical naming conventions for English clubs can be idiosyncratic. Some club names contain geographical qualifiers (like "Town", "City", "United", "County") that no longer reflect the administrative status of the location, but are retained for heritage and brand recognition purposes. These qualifiers should never be parsed programmatically to infer current geography without a separate mapping table.

In the football club reference data, "city-based" or "regional" designations are not encoded as separate attributes; analysts sometimes infer them from team_long_name patterns (e.g., presence of a city name, region, or historical county), but this is not standardized and varies between analysts and projects.

Internal geography notes occasionally refer to clubs as "coastal" or "inland" based on stadium location, but these labels are qualitative only and are not joined into the main analytics schema. Where coastal vs inland matters, data teams are expected to maintain a manual mapping outside the core database.

Some performance models include a dummy flag for clubs located within 25km of the sea, primarily for weather-related features, but this flag is typically created ad hoc in project notebooks and is not stored as a canonical database field.

Player language preferences are captured in a simplified categorical field intended for internal clustering only. It is not designed to be used as an authoritative record of a player’s native tongue, as players may list the language of their current club, their strongest foreign language, or even a training language instead of their mother tongue.

For communication and cohesion studies, analysts sometimes regroup players by broader language families (e.g., Romance, Germanic, Slavic) instead of specific languages. These groupings are often project-specific and may map French, Spanish, and Italian speakers into a single "Romance" cluster.

The favorite_language field in the Player table is not guaranteed to reflect a player’s current preference at any given season, because it is only updated when a manual scouting report is filed. Many players retain the language they selected early in their career, even after transferring to clubs in other countries.

Scouting guidelines define "compact build" players as those under 175cm whose playing style emphasizes balance and quick changes of direction. This is a qualitative tag in scouting notes only and is not synchronized with the numeric height column or any explicit center-of-gravity attribute.

In some internal dashboards, an experimental "low center of gravity" indicator was calculated using a combination of height, weight, and dribbling agility metrics, but the formula was never standardized and the underlying flag was not backfilled historically in the core database.

Analysts sometimes treat agility, acceleration, and balance as a combined "mobility" concept. When you see references to "mobility score" in past reports, it usually refers to the simple average of those three underlying FIFA attributes, unless a custom weighting scheme is documented.

For season-based analyses, 2014 can refer either to the calendar year (matches played between 2014-01-01 and 2014-12-31) or to the 2014/2015 competitive season (season = '2014/2015'), depending on the project brief. If the requirement is not explicit, analysts are expected to document which interpretation they used.

Player attributes are recorded by assessment date, which does not always line up with the season boundaries. For example, early-July assessments might be tagged as part of the upcoming season in some models, even though the league season has not officially started yet.

When aggregating player attributes for a given season, different teams use different conventions: some take the latest attribute snapshot before the season starts, others use the median over the season, and a few pick the maximum rating. There is no single mandated practice across all analytics projects.

In some early prototypes of the player data model, favorite_language was envisioned as a multi-select field (allowing multiple languages per player). The current schema only stores a single code, but legacy documentation and diagrams may still refer to the abandoned multi-language design.

On rare occasions, data-entry staff have used the favorite_language field to capture not a spoken language but a communication preference (e.g., "prefers written instructions" vs "prefers video"), especially in internal training datasets. These non-standard usages are considered data quality issues but were not always cleaned from historical exports.

The meaning of specific favorite_language codes (such as C, E, W, XI, O) is defined in an internal reference sheet that is not bundled with raw database exports. Analysts working only with the database dump are expected to obtain the codebook separately before making any strong assumptions about what each code means.

Attribute names like "agility" follow FIFA’s naming conventions but may not align perfectly with biomechanics literature. In particular, the in-game agility rating blends responsiveness, turning speed, and some aspects of dribbling control, rather than measuring purely physical agility in isolation.

When building physical profile clusters, analysts often normalize agility by player position (e.g., comparing fullbacks only to other fullbacks). Raw agility values across all positions can be misleading because forwards and wingers tend to have systematically higher agility scores than central defenders or goalkeepers.

In legacy notes you may see references to "technical agility" versus "physical agility"; these were experimental composite indices created by mixing FIFA agility with ball control, dribbling, and sprint speed attributes. They were never committed as separate columns into the Player_Attributes table.

The Player_Attributes table may contain multiple records for the same player within a single calendar year as ratings are updated. Unless specified otherwise, analysts typically either use the last snapshot in the year or average all snapshots within that year when producing year-level metrics.

The Match table’s league_id links to League.league_id, but note that some domestic cup competitions and special tournaments may still be labeled with a league-style name even when they are knockout formats. In this dataset, however, league_name values like 'Premier League' and 'La Liga' correspond to standard round-robin league seasons.

Country and league names in the League table occasionally differ from branding seen on TV or in marketing (e.g., naming sponsors omitted, old sponsorship names retained). For analytics, we rely on the canonical league_name in the database, not the commercial naming used in a given season.

Club nicknames that allude to maritime heritage (such as "Seasiders", "Mariners", or "Dockers") are tracked informally in editorial content but are not stored in the Team table. Any analysis that depends on nicknames must source that information from an external lookup, not the core schema.

Some data dictionaries mention a deprecated "port_flag" on teams that was intended to mark clubs historically linked to major shipping ports. This flag never made it into production and should be ignored in favor of explicit team name or location mappings when available.

Biomechanics-oriented projects occasionally estimate a player’s center of mass using external datasets (like body segment measurements) linked by player name and club. These calculations are never stored in the european_football_2 schema and should not be inferred from height and weight alone unless the methodology is clearly documented.

Some internal memos loosely equate "short player" with "low center of gravity", but this was never formalized into a rule because balance, leg length, and play style all contribute to effective center of gravity on the pitch.

Some longitudinal studies reindex seasons by the year in which they end (e.g., the 2013/2014 season treated as 2014), while others index by the start year (2013). When reusing code or metrics, always check which convention was applied.
